parts.content: Identify the glycolysis enzyme inhibited by $2$-DG.
parts.markscheme: - Hexokinase <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how this inhibition affects the early steps of glycolysis.
parts.markscheme:
- Hexokinase catalyses phosphorylation of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate (G-6-P) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phosphorylation helps trap glucose (as G-6-P) inside the cell/commits it to glycolysis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $2$-DG acts as a glucose analogue/competitive substrate for hexokinase <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phosphorylated $2$-DG (e.g., $2$-DG-6-phosphate) cannot be further metabolized normally, reducing glycolytic flux/halting early glycolysis <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: From the bar graph, compare ATP production from glycolysis in control and $2$-DG-treated cells (use approximate values or units shown).
parts.markscheme:
- Control glycolysis ATP production is about $2$ arbitrary units <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- $2$-DG-treated glycolysis ATP production is about $0.5$ arbitrary units (much lower than control) <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Glycolysis ATP production decreases markedly in $2$-DG-treated cells compared with control <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest why mitochondrial ATP production remains unchanged despite glycolysis inhibition.
parts.markscheme:
- $2$-DG inhibits glycolysis but does not directly inhibit mitochondrial electron transport chain/oxidative phosphorylation enzymes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cells can supply the citric acid (Krebs) cycle with acetyl-CoA/reducing equivalents from non-glucose sources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Alternative substrates such as fatty acids and/or glutamine can be oxidized to maintain respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mitochondria can continue to generate ATP as long as substrates and oxygen are available (so mitochondrial ATP output can be maintained) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify the molecule in the schematic that links glycolysis to the Krebs cycle.
parts.markscheme: - Pyruvate <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how this molecule is processed in aerobic conditions to allow continued ATP production.
parts.markscheme:
- Pyruvate is transported into the mitochondrion/matrix <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pyruvate is oxidatively decarboxylated (link reaction) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acetyl group combines with coenzyme A to form acetyl-CoA <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acetyl-CoA enters the citric acid (Krebs) cycle to support continued production of reduced NAD/ATP via oxidative phosphorylation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Reduced NAD is formed at multiple points in aerobic respiration. State two pathways shown in the schematic where reduced NAD is generated.
parts.markscheme:
- Any two from:
  - Glycolysis <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Link reaction (pyruvate oxidation/PDH complex) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Citric acid (Krebs) cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>

Note: award only if two pathways are stated. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how reduced NAD is used to drive ATP production in the mitochondrion.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced NAD (NADH) donates electrons to the electron transport chain (ETC) in the inner mitochondrial membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Electrons pass along carriers in the ETC in redox reactions, releasing energy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This energy is used to pump protons (H$^+$) across the inner membrane to create a proton gradient/proton motive force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Protons flow back through ATP synthase, driving phosphorylation of ADP to ATP (chemiosmosis) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles responsible for producing most of the cell's ATP through aerobic respiration. The inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM) contains proteins like ATP synthase and the respiratory chain complexes. The mitochondrial matrix (MM) houses enzymes involved in the Krebs cycle (TCA cycle), and mitochondria have their own DNA ($mtDNA$). Fatty acids and glucose can both contribute to $ATP$ production.

Disruption in mitochondrial structure or function can lead to reduced energy production and the activation of cell death pathways.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/qKgozqQ5KkQ7WoctxMW2c.jpeg)

Mitochondrial structure and metabolism

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/KHqqLyGhQVuFMq6MkuFKy.jpeg)

Consequences of mitochondrial dysfunction

parts.content: Identify two structural features of the mitochondrion shown (labelled) in Figure a. Explain how each is related to its function.
parts.markscheme:
- **Double membrane (OMM and IMM)**: allows compartmentalization and creates an intermembrane space so a proton gradient can be established for chemiosmosis/oxidative phosphorylation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Cristae / folded IMM**: increases surface area for attachment of respiratory chain complexes and $ATP$ synthase, increasing capacity for $ATP$ production <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (do not award beyond max):
- **IMM contains ETC/respiratory chain complexes and $ATP$ synthase** enabling oxidative phosphorylation
- **Matrix (MM)** contains enzymes for the TCA cycle, producing reduced cofactors for the ETC

parts.content: State the two major energy sources that enter the mitochondria for $ATP$ production, based on Figure a.
parts.markscheme:
- Glucose (entering mitochondrial metabolism as pyruvate / acetyl-CoA) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fatty acids (entering as fatty acyl-CoA for $\beta$-oxidation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (do not award beyond max):
- Both feed into formation of acetyl-CoA which enters the TCA cycle
- These sources provide electron carriers (e.g. NADH/FADH$_2$) for oxidative phosphorylation

parts.content: Cancer cells are shown to favor lactate production in Figure a. Explain why this results in less efficient $ATP$ production than aerobic respiration.
parts.markscheme:
- Lactate production is associated with anaerobic glycolysis, which yields a much lower net $ATP$ per glucose (e.g. 2 $ATP$) than aerobic respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aerobic respiration uses the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, producing much more $ATP$ per glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (do not award beyond max):
- Conversion of pyruvate to lactate regenerates NAD$^+$, allowing glycolysis to continue but bypasses oxidative phosphorylation
- Less energy is extracted because glucose is not fully oxidized to CO$_2$ (energy remains in lactate)

parts.content: With reference to Figure b, suggest one reason why cells have multiple ways of responding to mitochondrial dysfunction.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiple pathways provide flexibility so the response can match the type or severity of mitochondrial damage <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Different pathways allow the cell to respond to reversible damage (recovery/adaptation/mitophagy) versus irreversible damage requiring cell death (e.g. apoptosis) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline one role of mitochondrial DNA ($mtDNA$) and explain how its damage could affect cell function.
parts.markscheme:
- $mtDNA$ contains genes that code for some proteins required for oxidative phosphorylation/electron transport and mitochondrial function <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Damage to $mtDNA$ can lead to dysfunctional proteins/ETC complexes, reducing $ATP$ output and potentially triggering cell death pathways <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (do not award beyond max):
- Reduced efficiency of oxidative phosphorylation can increase electron leak and ROS production, further damaging cellular components
- Impaired respiration can disrupt cellular processes that require $ATP$ (e.g. active transport)

parts.content: Identify two cellular processes from Table 1 that involve intracellular movement and briefly describe how ATP is utilized in each.
parts.markscheme:
- Chromosome movement during mitosis: ATP provides energy for spindle microtubule dynamics and/or motor proteins to move chromosomes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vesicle transport (kinesin-mediated): ATP hydrolysis by kinesin powers movement of vesicles along microtubules. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (award credit for any relevant detail):
- ATP is used by motor proteins (e.g. kinesin/dynein) to generate force/motion. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ATP is required for assembly/disassembly of spindle fibres during mitosis. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1, calculate the percentage of total ATP consumption due to active transport. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Total ATP consumption rate = $1.2 + 0.9 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 3.1$ ($10^6$ molecules min$^{-1}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Percentage due to active transport = $(1.2/3.1)\times 100 \approx 38.7\%$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Accept answers between 38–39% (with correct working).

parts.content: Compare the ATP yield from glucose and palmitic acid as shown in Table 2. Explain why fatty acids like palmitic acid yield more ATP than glucose.
parts.markscheme:
- Palmitic acid yields more ATP per molecule (106) than glucose (30). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fatty acids are more reduced (higher proportion of C–H bonds / fewer O atoms) than glucose. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore oxidation produces more reduced coenzymes (more NADH and $\text{FADH}_2$) during $\beta$-oxidation and the Krebs cycle. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (award credit for any relevant detail):
- More acetyl-CoA molecules are produced from a long-chain fatty acid, feeding multiple turns of the Krebs cycle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- More NADH/$\text{FADH}_2$ leads to more ATP produced by oxidative phosphorylation/ETC. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of ATP hydrolysis in powering the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$ pump during active transport.
parts.markscheme:
- ATP is hydrolysed and a phosphate is transferred to the pump (phosphorylation), providing energy for a conformational change. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This enables transport of ions against their concentration gradients (typically $3\text{Na}^+$ out and $2\text{K}^+$ in per ATP). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (award credit for any relevant detail):
- Dephosphorylation returns the protein to its original conformation to reset the cycle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The pump is an ATPase/antiporter involved in maintaining membrane potential. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how the hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and inorganic phosphate releases energy for cellular processes.
parts.markscheme:
- Hydrolysis of ATP to ADP + $\text{P}_i$ releases energy due to formation of more stable products (lower free energy). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The released energy can be coupled to endergonic reactions by phosphorylating substrates/proteins, making them more reactive. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This coupling powers cellular work (e.g. active transport, biosynthesis, muscle contraction/motor protein movement). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (award credit for any relevant detail):
- ATP hydrolysis can drive conformational changes in proteins. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy is released when new bonds form in ADP and $\text{P}_i$ and/or due to reduced electrostatic repulsion after hydrolysis. <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: Protons move into the matrix down their gradient, driving phosphorylation of ADP to ATP.
options.markscheme: Correct: chemiosmosis through ATP synthase uses the proton motive force (electrochemical gradient) to power ATP synthesis in the matrix.

options.content: Protons are pumped from the matrix to the intermembrane space by ATP synthase, using ATP energy.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: proton pumping from matrix to intermembrane space is done by electron transport chain complexes (I, III, IV), not ATP synthase, and it uses redox energy, not ATP.

options.content: Protons diffuse through the inner membrane lipid bilayer into the matrix, producing ATP directly.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the inner mitochondrial membrane is largely impermeable to $H^+$; protons return via ATP synthase, and ATP is formed via catalysis at ATP synthase rather than by direct diffusion.

options.content: Protons move from the intermembrane space into the matrix through ATP synthase, reducing $NAD^+$ to NADH.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: proton flow through ATP synthase drives ATP formation; $NAD^+$ reduction to NADH occurs in earlier stages (e.g., Krebs cycle), not at ATP synthase.

parts.content: Explain why $\text{CO}_2$ production increases during exercise.

parts.content: Compare and contrast aerobic and anaerobic respiration.
parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Explain the roles of chloroplasts and mitochondria in energy conversion.
parts.markscheme:

- chloroplasts use $\text{CO}_2$ and water to produce organic compounds/glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>
- mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to break down glucose and generate ATP <marks>1 mark</marks>
- mitochondria release $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ as waste products of respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- both organelles possess a double membrane and contain their own DNA and 70S ribosomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- chloroplasts are found in autotrophic organisms (plants/algae), while mitochondria are found in almost all eukaryotic cells <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare ATP production in aerobic and anaerobic respiration using the table.
parts.markscheme:
- aerobic respiration produces more ATP per glucose than anaerobic conditions ($36$ vs $2$ ATP molecules per glucose molecule) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- aerobic yield is about $18\times$ higher ($\frac{36}{2}=18$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- aerobic respiration includes oxidative phosphorylation (electron transport chain) which generates most ATP; anaerobic conditions rely on glycolysis only <marks>1 mark</marks>
- oxygen availability allows maximum ATP yield because oxygen is the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why ATP production is lower in anaerobic conditions.
parts.markscheme:
- in anaerobic conditions, ATP is produced only by glycolysis, giving a net yield of $2$ ATP per glucose molecule <marks>1 mark</marks>
- without oxygen, the electron transport chain cannot operate, so oxidative phosphorylation cannot occur <marks>1 mark</marks>
- NADH cannot be reoxidized via the electron transport chain; fermentation regenerates NAD$^+$ but produces no additional ATP beyond glycolysis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- no proton gradient is maintained across the inner mitochondrial membrane, so ATP synthase produces little/no ATP from chemiosmosis <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: It accepts electrons and protons to form water.
options.markscheme: Oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor in the electron transport chain; it combines with electrons and $H^+$ to form water.

options.markscheme: NADH is oxidized by complex I, but oxygen accepts electrons at complex IV, not complex I.

options.content: It is the final electron acceptor in glycolysis, producing water in the cytoplasm.
options.markscheme: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and does not use oxygen; oxygen functions in the mitochondrial electron transport chain.

parts.content: Outline the key stages of glycolysis.
parts.markscheme:
- occurs in the cytoplasm of the cell <marks>1 mark</marks>
- glucose is phosphorylated using ATP <marks>1 mark</marks>
- glucose is split into two 3-carbon molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\text{NAD}^+$ is reduced to $\text{NADH}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ATP is produced via substrate-level phosphorylation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ends with two molecules of pyruvate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- does not require oxygen <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the products of aerobic respiration and their functions in the cell.
parts.markscheme:
- ATP is the main energy carrier used to power cellular processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ATP fuels activities like active transport and muscle contraction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- carbon dioxide is a waste gas produced during the Krebs cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- carbon dioxide is excreted by diffusion into the bloodstream and out through the lungs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- water is formed at the end of the electron transport chain when oxygen accepts electrons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- water can be used in metabolic reactions or excreted depending on the organism’s needs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- heat is released as a by-product and helps maintain body temperature in endotherms <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the process of ATP generation in aerobic respiration.
parts.markscheme:
- occurs in mitochondria <marks>1 mark</marks>
- electrons from $\text{NADH}$ and $\text{FADH}_2$ enter electron transport chain (ETC) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- energy used to pump protons ($\text{H}^+$) across inner membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- proton gradient forms across membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- protons flow back through ATP synthase <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ATP synthase phosphorylates ADP to ATP <marks>1 mark</marks>
- oxygen is final electron acceptor, forms water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- most ATP made in oxidative phosphorylation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- process is more efficient than anaerobic respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:

| | Net $CO_2$ molecules produced | Net $NADH + H^+$ molecules produced | Carbon-based product formed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | 1 | 1 | Acetyl CoA |
| **B** | 2 | 0 | Lactate |
| **C** | 2 | 2 | Acetyl CoA |
| **D** | 2 | 2 | Citrate |

options.markscheme: <note>A identifies the stroma, which contains the enzymes for the Calvin cycle but not ATP synthase.</note>
<note>B identifies the chloroplast envelope (outer membrane region), which provides protection and allows transport but does not contain ATP synthase.</note>
<note>C identifies the thylakoid membrane (of the thylakoid/granum), where the electron transport chain and ATP synthase are located.</note>
<note>D labels regions other than the thylakoid membrane in this diagram; ATP synthase is embedded in the thylakoid membrane, not in the stroma or envelope.</note>

parts.content: Describe the role of autotrophs in the initial stage of energy flow through a terrestrial ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
a. sunlight is the initial/primary source of energy for most ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>

b. autotrophs/producers use chlorophyll or other photosynthetic pigments to capture light energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

c. light energy is converted into chemical energy via the process of photosynthesis <marks>1 mark</marks>

d. chemical energy is used to synthesize organic carbon compounds/biomass/glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>

e. this energy/biomass is then made available to heterotrophs/consumers through feeding <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Contrast the movement of energy with the movement of inorganic nutrients within a community.
parts.markscheme:
a. energy flows linearly through an ecosystem whereas nutrients cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>

b. energy enters as light and is eventually lost to the environment as heat <marks>1 mark</marks>

c. heat energy cannot be converted into other forms of energy/cannot be recycled by organisms <marks>1 mark</marks>

d. nutrients (such as carbon, nitrogen, or phosphorus) are present in finite amounts <marks>1 mark</marks>

e. saprotrophs/decomposers break down dead organic matter to return nutrients to the soil/abiotic environment for reuse by producers <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the number of trophic levels in a natural food chain is typically limited to four or five.
parts.markscheme:
a. energy transfer between trophic levels is inefficient/only approximately 10% is passed on <marks>1 mark</marks>

b. energy is lost as heat from living organisms during the process of cellular respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

c. energy is used by organisms for metabolic processes such as movement, growth, or homeostasis <marks>1 mark</marks>

d. not all of an organism is consumed by the predator in the next level (e.g., bones, woody stems) <marks>1 mark</marks>

e. some consumed material is indigestible and is lost in feces/egestion <marks>1 mark</marks>

f. energy is also lost in excretion (e.g., nitrogenous wastes) <marks>1 mark</marks>

g. total biomass decreases significantly at higher trophic levels <marks>1 mark</marks>

h. at the top of the chain, there is insufficient energy remaining to support a viable population of another trophic level <marks>1 mark</marks>
